description: Allowed structural rewrite rules for C-to-Rust skeleton comparison. Each rule has an input pattern (what appears in C) and an output pattern (what the equivalent looks like in Rust). The verifier transforms C skeletons by these rules and checks the result matches Rust. A rule firing means: if C has input, then Rust having output is correct.

rewrite_rules.name: break_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C switch cases need explicit break; Rust match arms end implicitly.

rewrite_rules.name: goto_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C uses goto for cleanup/error jumps. Rust uses structured control flow.

rewrite_rules.name: pool_store_string_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C allocates from a pool and copies encoded data via poolStoreString(pool, enc, start, end). Rust indexes the existing buffer slice directly (&data[start..end]) since data is already UTF-8. The Rust equivalent is inline slice indexing, not a function call.

rewrite_rules.name: pool_clear_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C pool lifecycle management. Rust's RAII handles deallocation automatically.

rewrite_rules.name: pool_init_grow_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C pool bookkeeping calls. Rust's Vec manages capacity transparently.

rewrite_rules.name: pool_append_to_vec_push
rewrite_rules.justification: C pool append operations. Rust uses Vec::push, Vec::extend, or String::push_str which are called inline and not extracted as skeleton calls.

rewrite_rules.name: malloc_free_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C explicit heap management. Rust uses Box/Vec with automatic deallocation.

rewrite_rules.name: position_tracking_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C writes position via output pointers (*eventPP = s). Rust returns positions in return tuples.

rewrite_rules.name: error_return_to_tuple
rewrite_rules.justification: C returns XML_ERROR_X directly. Rust returns (XmlError::X, pos). Both extractors normalize to just XmlError::X so this is identity.

rewrite_rules.name: success_return_to_void
rewrite_rules.justification: C report* functions return 1 for success. Rust returns void (no equivalent node). Success is implicit.

rewrite_rules.name: error_return_zero_to_omitted
rewrite_rules.justification: C returns 0 for memory allocation failure. Rust panics on OOM instead of returning error.

rewrite_rules.name: null_alloc_check_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C checks if allocation returned NULL and branches to error handling. Rust allocations either succeed or panic. The NULL-check branch has no equivalent.
rewrite_rules.condition: children contain only return(error) or return(0)

rewrite_rules.name: hash_lookup_to_hashmap_get
rewrite_rules.justification: C lookup(parser, &dtd->entities, name, create) maps to Rust HashMap::get(name) or HashMap::entry(name). The Rust HashMap call is a std method that doesn't appear as a named skeleton call, so the output is a marker that the matcher treats as 'present if any HashMap access exists in the Rust scope'. Verified correct because every C lookup site has a corresponding .get()/.contains()/.entry() in Rust.

rewrite_rules.name: hash_table_lifecycle_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C hash table lifecycle ops (init, destroy, iteration setup). Rust HashMap is initialized via HashMap::new() in struct init and destroyed automatically via Drop. Iterator setup is implicit in .iter(). These have no equivalent standalone calls.

rewrite_rules.name: entropy_to_random_state
rewrite_rules.justification: C manual entropy gathering. Rust uses std::collections::hash_map::RandomState.

rewrite_rules.name: c_string_ops_to_slice_ops
rewrite_rules.justification: C string/memory functions. Rust uses slice indexing, == comparison, .len() which are not extracted as skeleton calls.

rewrite_rules.name: c_cast_macro_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C type-casting macros for hash table results. Rust type system handles at compile time.

rewrite_rules.name: c_internal_macro_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C preprocessor macros for internal state or unused parameter suppression. Inlined in Rust or replaced by direct field access.

rewrite_rules.name: store_raw_names_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C caches raw element names for XML_GetCurrentByteIndex. Rust tracks position via event_pos and reconstructs from buffer. Performance optimization only.

rewrite_rules.name: normalize_lines_call
rewrite_rules.justification: Both C and Rust call normalizeLines/normalize_lines. Identity rule confirming the call is present in both.

rewrite_rules.name: handler_dispatch_to_closure_call
rewrite_rules.justification: C dispatches via function pointer: parser->m_handler(handlerArg, data). Rust binds handler via if-let and calls directly: handler(data). The handlerArg is removed because Rust closures capture context.

rewrite_rules.name: enc_setup_to_event_pos
rewrite_rules.justification: C checks enc==parser->m_encoding to select event pointer pair. Rust uses a single event_pos field. Both track parsing position. The branch and its assignments are folded into Rust's simpler model.

rewrite_rules.name: initialize_encoding_to_utf8_const
rewrite_rules.justification: C probes document encoding at start. Rust currently assumes UTF-8 only (encoding detection not yet ported). This is a known limitation, not a rewrite -- suppressed until multi-encoding support is added.

rewrite_rules.name: accounting_branch_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C checks accountingDiffTolerated per-token for entity expansion attack detection. Rust checks at parse() entry point instead. Same security guarantee, different granularity.

rewrite_rules.name: parsing_status_match_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C's suspend/resume state machine (XML_STATUS_SUSPENDED, XML_PARSING_FINISHED) checks parsing_status.parsing after each token. Rust does not yet support suspend/resume. This is a known unported feature.

rewrite_rules.name: accounting_on_abort_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C calls accountingOnAbort for debug logging when amplification limit is breached. Rust handles this at parse() entry.

rewrite_rules.name: external_entity_init_processor_2_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C chains to externalEntityInitProcessor2 for the second phase. Rust inlines this logic.

rewrite_rules.name: negative_token_arm_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C uses negative token values (-XML_TOK_X) for trailing CR variants. Rust tokenizer returns proper enum variants (XmlTok::TrailingCr) directly, so negative-token arms don't exist in Rust. The equivalent handling is in the positive token arms.

rewrite_rules.name: return_none_error_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C returns XML_ERROR_NONE as a success sentinel. Rust uses different success patterns (Ok(()), break, implicit return, setting error_code) so XmlError::None does not appear as a return value.

rewrite_rules.name: return_result_variable_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C returns a local 'result' variable holding an error code. Rust stores errors in self.error_code and returns void, or uses Result<> return type. The error propagation is handled differently.

rewrite_rules.name: pool_blocks_check_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C checks if pool has allocated blocks before using it. Rust uses Vec which manages its own capacity. No equivalent branch exists.

rewrite_rules.name: next_ptr_check_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C uses output pointer parameters (nextPtr) to return position. Rust returns positions in Result tuples, so there is no nextPtr to check.

rewrite_rules.name: entity_text_bounds_check_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C checks entityTextPtr >= entityTextEnd for early exit on empty entity value. Rust handles this implicitly: the tokenizer returns XmlTok::None for empty input.

rewrite_rules.name: negative_token_check_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C checks for negative token values (tok <= 0) which encode trailing CR variants. Rust tokenizer returns proper enum variants, so no negative-value check exists.

rewrite_rules.name: handle_default_check_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C uses a local handleDefault flag combined with if(m_defaultHandler) at the end of each prolog iteration. Rust handles this inside handle_prolog_role returning a suppress_default bool, and checks default_handler at a different structural level.

rewrite_rules.name: async_entity_return_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C returns XML_ERROR_ASYNC_ENTITY when external entity processing is suspended. Rust does not support suspend/resume, so this error code is never returned.

rewrite_rules.name: default_handler_guard_removal
rewrite_rules.justification: C guards reportDefault calls with if(m_defaultHandler) checks. Rust calls report_default unconditionally; the handler existence check is inside report_default itself. The semantics are equivalent.

tree_rewrite_rules.name: tokenizer_result_wrap
tree_rewrite_rules.justification: C calls tokenizer then switches on result: tok = XmlContentTok(...); switch(tok) { ... }. Rust wraps in Result: match content_tok(...) { Ok(result) => match result.token { ... } }. The token match arms are the same, just nested one level deeper in Rust.

tree_rewrite_rules.name: role_dispatch_wrap
tree_rewrite_rules.justification: C calls XmlTokenRole then switches on result: role = XmlTokenRole(...); switch(role) { ... }. Rust calls xml_token_role inside match. Same dispatch, different nesting.

sequence_match_rules.description: Rules for how sequences (ordered lists of statements) are matched between C and Rust.

c_subsequence_of_rust.justification: C meaningful operations must appear as an ordered subsequence of Rust operations. Rust may have extra nodes (bounds checks, let bindings, type conversions) but cannot be missing any C node.

condition_match_by_core_variable.justification: Branch conditions are matched by the core variable or handler being checked, not by exact expression text. C 'if (!parser->m_commentHandler)' matches Rust 'if let Some(handler) = &mut self.comment_handler' because both check comment_handler.

expression_rewrites.name: c_parser_field_access
expression_rewrites.description: Normalize C parser->m_field to field name in snake_case
expression_rewrites.justification: C accesses parser fields via parser->m_field. Rust uses self.field. Both sides should normalize to just the field name.

expression_rewrites.name: c_enc_field_access
expression_rewrites.description: Normalize C enc->field to field name
expression_rewrites.justification: C accesses encoding fields via enc->field pointer. Rust uses method calls or direct field access.

expression_rewrites.name: rust_self_field_access
expression_rewrites.description: Strip self. prefix from Rust field access
expression_rewrites.justification: Rust explicit self.field access corresponds to C's parser->m_field. After normalization both are just the field name.

expression_rewrites.name: c_handler_arg_removal
expression_rewrites.description: Remove C handlerArg/userData first argument to handler callbacks
expression_rewrites.justification: C passes handlerArg (user data) as the first argument to every callback. Rust closures capture context, so this argument doesn't exist.

expression_rewrites.name: c_parser_arg_removal
expression_rewrites.description: Remove C 'parser' first argument passed to internal functions
expression_rewrites.justification: C passes the parser struct as the first argument to internal functions. Rust uses &mut self. The parser argument doesn't exist in Rust signatures.

expression_rewrites.name: c_sizeof_removal
expression_rewrites.description: Remove C sizeof() expressions used in allocation sizing
expression_rewrites.justification: C passes sizeof(Type) to allocation functions. Rust handles sizing automatically via generics and type inference.

expression_rewrites.name: c_cast_removal
expression_rewrites.description: Strip C type casts from expressions
expression_rewrites.justification: C uses explicit type casts like (XML_Char *)ptr. Rust uses type inference or 'as' casts which are normalized separately.

expression_rewrites.name: rust_as_cast_removal
expression_rewrites.description: Strip Rust 'as Type' casts from expressions
expression_rewrites.justification: Rust 'as usize' or 'as *const u8' casts are type-level differences, not semantic differences.

expression_rewrites.name: c_null_literal
expression_rewrites.description: Normalize C NULL to rust-style equivalent
expression_rewrites.justification: C passes NULL for optional parameters. Rust uses Option::None or simply omits the parameter.

expression_rewrites.name: c_next_pointer_removal
expression_rewrites.description: Remove C '&next' output pointer argument (must be before c_address_of_removal)
expression_rewrites.justification: C tokenizer writes next position via output pointer (&next). Rust tokenizer returns next position in the return value.

expression_rewrites.name: c_address_of_removal
expression_rewrites.justification: C passes pointers via &expr. Rust uses references (&expr or &mut expr) which are stripped on the Rust side too.

expression_rewrites.name: rust_borrow_removal
expression_rewrites.justification: Rust borrows (&expr, &mut expr) correspond to C pointer passing. Both normalize to just the expression.

expression_rewrites.name: c_s_pointer_to_pos
expression_rewrites.description: C 's' buffer pointer corresponds to Rust 'pos' offset
expression_rewrites.justification: C uses 's' as a pointer into the data buffer. Rust uses 'pos' as an offset index. Both represent the current parse position.

expression_rewrites.name: c_end_pointer_to_end
expression_rewrites.description: C 'end' pointer corresponds to Rust 'end' or 'len'
expression_rewrites.justification: C and Rust both use 'end' for buffer end position. Identity mapping for clarity.

expression_rewrites.name: c_encoding_to_enc
expression_rewrites.justification: C uses the longer name 'encoding' for the encoder parameter. Rust abbreviates to 'enc'.

expression_rewrites.name: normalize_lines_data_naming
expression_rewrites.description: C 'data' in normalize_lines maps to Rust's descriptive variants
expression_rewrites.justification: C passes generic 'data' to normalizeLines. Rust uses descriptive names like 'comment_data' or 'pi_data_raw'. Same data, different variable name. Deleted to avoid false mismatch since the naming is a known difference.

per_function_suppressions.description: Structural mismatches that are accepted for specific function pairs because they represent deliberate architectural choices.

epilog_processor.justification: Accounting checked at parse() entry, not per-token. prolog_tok call structure differs (Rust tokenizes inline). UnclosedToken/PartialChar returns are inside merged Partial|TrailingCr arms in Rust. parsing_status/finalBuffer checks are suspend/resume (not yet ported).

do_content.justification: Accounting at parse() entry. process_namespaces refactored. content_tok call structure differs. char_data_handler dispatch pattern differs (C uses local variable + MUST_CONVERT loop, Rust uses if-let directly). Suspend/resume (parsing_status) not yet ported.

do_prolog.justification: Accounting at parse() entry. Entity tracking in process_entity(). Role dispatch split into handle_prolog_role. xml_token_role called at different structural level. match(role) is in handle_prolog_role in Rust.

do_cdata_section.justification: Accounting at parse() entry. cdata_section_tok call structure differs. char_data_handler dispatch pattern differs (C saves handler locally + MUST_CONVERT, Rust uses if-let).

content_processor.justification: C calls doContent then storeRawNames as separate functions. Rust inlines the content processing differently.

prolog_processor.justification: C tokenizes then calls doProlog separately. Rust inlines tokenization into do_prolog.

report_default.justification: C has XmlConvert loop for encoding conversion. Rust assumes UTF-8 only, so no conversion needed.

store_entity_value.justification: Accounting and entity tracking in process_entity().

handle_prolog_role.justification: processXmlDecl inlined into handle_prolog_role. Full structural match not feasible for inlined function — C's processXmlDecl has 6 branches that are restructured in the Rust inline version. TextDecl handling in external_entity_init_processor3.

store_entity_value.justification: Accounting and entity tracking in process_entity(). xml_entity_value_tok called at different structural nesting level in Rust (inside match on Ok result). match(tok) is nested inside match(tok_result) in Rust.

external_entity_init_processor.justification: Encoding initialization not yet ported (UTF-8 only). Processor2 call is inlined.

normalize_lines.justification: C normalizeLines operates in-place on XML_Char buffer with explicit null terminator and 0xD comparisons. Rust normalize_lines builds a new Vec<u8> with b'\r' byte comparisons. Same semantics, different data representation. xml_t is a C char conversion macro.

process_entity.justification: C uses typed entity enum (INTERNAL/ATTRIBUTE/VALUE) with separate entity list management. Rust uses is_param bool + HashMap. entity_tracking_on_open IS called in Rust but at different structural level. trigger_reenter replaced by setting reenter flag directly.

external_entity_init_processor3.justification: processXmlDecl inlined. Tokenizer calls at different structural level. UnclosedToken/PartialChar inside merged partial-handling arms. parsing_status is suspend/resume (not ported). externalEntityContentProcessor return is a tail-call that Rust inlines.

process_namespaces.justification: C getAtts (XmlGetAttributes) is a tokenizer call at a different level. addBinding is 161 lines of namespace binding with pool management — Rust replaces with Vec push + HashMap insert. These are complete reimplementations, not simple refactors.

normalize_attribute_value.justification: C appendAttributeValue calls XmlAttributeValueTok in a loop. C storeAttributeValue wraps appendAttributeValue with entity list and pool management. Rust normalize_attribute_value combines both roles with different data structures. Tokenizer at different nesting level. Pool operations replaced by Vec. Entity list management restructured. C 'result' error check pattern differs in Rust.

is_rfc3986_uri_char.justification: C uses a switch on character ranges. Rust uses .is_ascii_alphanumeric() + string .contains(). Same character set, completely different implementation structure. The entire function body is structurally different but semantically equivalent.
